title: Behçet Disease
oneLiner: Systemic vasculitis of unknown cause, classically in young adults along the “Silk Road,” presenting with recurrent oral/genital ulcers, uveitis, and skin lesions – a great mimic that can blind or burst.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 28-year-old man of Turkish descent presents to the eye casualty with acute red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision. On closer questioning, he reports recurrent painful mouth ulcers (“canker sores” that come every few months) and occasional painful “sores” on his scrotum that heal with white scarring. He also noticed erythematous tender nodules on his shins last month. Fluorescein exam shows anterior uveitis with hypopyon. A pathergy test (skin prick with a sterile needle) produces a sterile pustule at 48 hours.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- Young adult (20-40 years), Mediterranean/Middle Eastern ancestry → **high pre-test probability**; Silk Road geography is a classic clue.
- Acute red eye + hypopyon (pus in anterior chamber) → **non‑infectious uveitis** – Behçet is one of the few causes of hypopyon without infection.
- Recurrent painful mouth ulcers (oral aphthae) – **major diagnostic criterion**; look for multiple, deep, well-defined ulcers (not the trivial ones you see in common aphthous).
- Genital ulcers (scrotal, labial) – **second major criterion**; often deeper than oral ones, heal with scarring (unlike herpes).
- Erythema nodosum on shins → **skin manifestation** of Behçet – painful, red, subcutaneous nodules, not nodules of erythema induratum.
- Positive pathergy test → **specific but not sensitive** hyper-reactivity of skin to minor trauma; a key pattern clue in the right context.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Neurologic variant (Neuro-Behçet):** A 35-year-old with headache, hemiparesis, and meningism – looks like stroke or meningitis. The clue is a history of recurrent oral ulcers that the patient “always had.” Imaging shows brainstem or basal ganglia lesions.
2. **Vascular variant:** A 30-year-old with unilateral leg swelling (DVT) or a pulsatile abdominal mass (abdominal aortic aneurysm). Behçet is one of the few causes of both arterial aneurysm and venous thrombosis. Clue: recurrent oral ulcers and positive pathergy.
3. **GI variant:** A 25-year-old with abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and mouth ulcers – mimics Crohn’s. However, Behçet ulcers are usually deeper, punched-out, and more likely to perforate. Look for other organ involvement (eyes, skin, joints).

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**
Behçet disease is a chronic, relapsing‑remitting systemic vasculitis that can inflame any size blood vessel (arteries and veins, small to large). It is not a classic autoimmune disease (no autoantibodies) but an **autoinflammatory** condition driven by a hyperactive innate immune system attacking the vessel wall.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Rare in northern Europe, common in Turkey (prevalence ~400/100,000), but you will see it in diverse populations due to migration patterns. Miss it and the patient can become blind (untreated uveitis) or develop fatal vascular rupture.
- **Stakes:** Blindness (leading cause of morbidity), CNS involvement (parenchymal disease or dural sinus thrombosis), large-vessel aneurysms (rupture), and GI perforation.
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking oral ulcers for “aphthous stomatitis” (common), missing genital ulcers because patients don’t volunteer them, and treating “red eye” as conjunctivitis while missing uveitis.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:**
Behçet is a **neutrophilic vasculitis** – the neutrophil is the main effector cell. There is a genetic predisposition (HLA‑B51 is the strongest risk allele, particularly in Silk Road populations). Environmental triggers (e.g., oral streptococcal antigens, herpes simplex virus, heat shock proteins) are thought to initiate an exaggerated inflammatory response in genetically susceptible individuals.

**Mechanism → sign:**

- **Oral/genital ulcers:** Activated T cells (Th1/Th17) and neutrophils infiltrate the mucosa. The over‑exuberant release of cytokines (TNF‑α, IL‑1, IL‑17) causes deep, painful ulcerations. Why so recurrent? The abnormal immune response never fully switches off → relapsing course.
- **Uveitis (especially hypopyon):** Neutrophils migrate into the anterior chamber → pus (hypopyon). This is a **sterile** inflammation – don’t try to culture it; it’s an attack on the uveal tract.
- **Skin lesions:** Pathergy – the exaggerated response to needle prick reflects the skin’s hyperreactivity: neutrophils rush to the trauma site and produce a pustule. Erythema nodosum results from septal panniculitis (inflammation of fat septa). Papulopustular lesions (acneiform) are not from Propionibacterium but from sterile neutrophilic infiltration.
- **Vascular involvement:** The vasa vasorum is attacked → vessel wall weakened → aneurysm (arterial) or intimal inflammation → thrombosis (venous). The **coexistence** of arterial aneurysm and venous thrombosis is a hallmark that helps differentiate from conventional DVT (where arterial disease is rare).
- **CNS involvement:** Small‑vessel vasculitis in the brainstem, basal ganglia, white matter → focal neurological deficits. Meningeal inflammation → headache, neck stiffness (but CSF is usually bland with neutrophils).
- **GI involvement:** Vasculitic ulcers in the intestine (especially ileocecal) resemble Crohn’s but are deeper, punched‑out, and more likely to perforate (because the vessel wall is directly damaged).

**Connecting to pattern clues:**
- The hypopyon uveitis is a direct consequence of neutrophil chemotaxis into the eye.
- The pathergy test is a recapitulation of the skin’s hypersensitive neutrophil response.
- Recurrent oral/genital ulcers are the “low‑grade hum” of chronic TNF‑α activation that flares cyclically.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Cause / Trigger      | Category          | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| HLA‑B51 positivity   | Genetic (strong)  | “Patient of Turkish/Japanese/Middle Eastern origin” |
| Family history       | Familial          | “Sibling also has recurrent mouth ulcers and iritis” |
| Oral streptococcal infection (e.g., Streptococcus sanguinis) | Infectious trigger | “Had a dental procedure 2–4 weeks before flare” |
| HSV infection        | Viral trigger     | “Recurrent cold sores and then developed genital ulcers” |
| Heat shock proteins  | Stress / immune   | “Flare after major surgery or fever” |
| Smoking              | Environmental     | “Symptoms worse when smoking” (but effect is controversial) |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic, but with a strong genetic component (HLA‑B51 positive in ~50–80% of patients, depending on ethnicity). No single trigger is identified in most cases; it’s a **multifactorial** interplay.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (International Study Group):**
- **Required:** Recurrent oral aphthous ulcers (≥3 episodes in 12 months)
- **Plus any 2 of:**
  1. Recurrent genital ulceration
  2. Eye lesions (anterior/posterior uveitis, retinal vasculitis)
  3. Skin lesions (erythema nodosum, papulopustular lesions, pathergy positivity)
  4. Positive pathergy test (sterile pustule at 48h after needle prick)

No single lab test confirms it – it’s clinical.

| Test                     | What it shows                   | Why you order it                          | How to interpret                                                                 |
|--------------------------|---------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Pathergy test            | Skin hyperreactivity            | Specific (but sensitivity variable)       | Pustule at 48h → positive. Note: very low sensitivity in Northern Europeans.     |
| Ophthalmoscopy/slit lamp | Uveitis, retinal vasculitis     | To detect asymptomatic eye involvement    | Hypopyon = acute anterior uveitis. Retinal haemorrhages = vasculitis.            |
| MRI brain (if neuro sx)  | Brainstem/basal ganglia lesions | To diagnose Neuro-Behçet                  | T2 hyperintensities in midbrain, pons, basal ganglia (not typical MS pattern).   |
| MRV (if headache)        | Dural sinus thrombosis          | Suspect CNS venous occlusion              | Empty delta sign on MRV = superior sagittal sinus thrombosis.                    |
| HLA-B51                  | Genetic risk marker             | Supports diagnosis but not confirmatory   | Positive in ~50% of patients; negative does NOT rule out.                        |
| ESR/CRP                  | Inflammation                    | Monitor disease activity                  | Usually very elevated during flares.                                             |
| ANCA / ANA / dsDNA       | Exclusion                      | Rule out GPA (ANCA) and SLE (ANA/dsDNA)   | Negative in Behçet (helps differential).                                         |

**What single finding confirms?** None in isolation. The combination of oral ulcers + genital ulcers + uveitis is pathognomonic. **Pathergy positivity** in a patient with uveitis is highly specific.

**What single finding rules out?** A negative pathergy test does not rule out – only about 60% of patients (in Turkey) are positive. Lack of eye involvement in a patient with only mucocutaneous disease does not rule out Behçet, but it shifts the diagnosis away if more than a few years without eye inflammation.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **If acute uveitis (painful red eye, hypopyon):** Urgent ophthalmology referral. Topical corticosteroid (e.g., prednisolone acetate 1% one drop hourly) and cycloplegic (atropine 1% to prevent synechiae). **Do not** give antibiotics – it’s sterile.
- **If suspected arterial aneurysm (painful pulsatile mass, back pain):** Immediate vascular surgery consultation. CTA or ultrasound. **Do not** start anticoagulation – risk of rupture.
- **If suspected acute CNS vasculitis (headache, focal deficit, meningism):** Urgent MRI/MRV. Start IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily × 5 days.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Mucocutaneous disease (oral/genital ulcers, skin lesions):**
  - Colchicine 500–600 mcg PO BD – first line, reduces frequency. (Mechanism: inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis.)
  - Topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% paste for oral ulcers, clobetasol cream for genital).
  - If colchicine fails: dapsone 50–100 mg PO daily (check G6PD) or azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day.
- **Active eye disease (uveitis, retinal vasculitis):**
  - Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 2–4 weeks) plus a steroid-sparing agent.
  - Azathioprine 75–100 mg PO daily (2 mg/kg) is first-line for eye disease. Cyclosporine A (3–5 mg/kg/day) or TNF inhibitors (infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then q8weeks) for refractory cases.
- **Major vessel thrombosis (DVT, dural sinus thrombosis):**
  - **Controversial area.** Inflammation-driven thrombus → anticoagulation alone is insufficient. Treat with steroids (prednisolone 40–60 mg/day) and immunosuppressants (azathioprine or cyclophosphamide). Anticoagulation is debated – current guidelines recommend immunosuppression first; add anticoagulation only if no aneurysm and with caution.
- **GI involvement (pain, diarrhoea, ulcers):**
  - Corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day). Anti-TNF (infliximab) is effective. **Avoid NSAIDs** – they can cause perforation.

**Long-term:**
- **Maintenance:** Colchicine for mucocutaneous. For organ-threatening disease (eye, CNS, vasculature), maintain on azathioprine + low-dose prednisolone. The goal is to wean steroids to <10 mg/day by 3–6 months.
- **Anti-TNF agents** (infliximab, adalimumab) are second/third line but effective for all severe manifestations.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - **Start anticoagulation for DVT without first controlling inflammation** – risk of paradoxical rupture of arterial aneurysms.
  - **Use high-dose steroids for months without steroid-sparing** – avoid osteopenia, avascular necrosis.
  - **Prescribe colchicine in renal failure without dose adjustment** – toxicity (diarrhoea, bone marrow suppression).
- **Pregnancy:** Disease often improves. Colchicine is safe. Azathioprine is used with caution. Avoid mycophenolate and cyclophosphamide.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature               | Behçet                          | Crohn’s disease (GI)           | Herpes simplex (genital)      | Reactive arthritis (Reiter’s)   | Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Oral ulcers           | Painful, recurrent, multiple    | Painful, deep aphthous-like    | Painful vesicles on lips      | Shallow, painless?               | Typically painless, “lupus” ulcers |
| Genital ulcers        | Deep, scar-forming              | Perianal fissures/fistulas     | Clusters of vesicular ulcers  | Usually none (balanitis circinata)| Rare                                |
| Eye involvement       | Anterior uveitis, hypopyon, retinal vasculitis | Episcleritis (rare) | Keratoconjunctivitis (dendritic) | Conjunctivitis, iritis      | Retinal vasculitis, but no hypopyon |
| Skin lesions          | Erythema nodosum, papulopustular, pathergy | Pyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum | Vesicular rash | Keratoderma blenorrhagicum, circinate balanitis | Malar rash, discoid, photosensitivity |
| Joint involvement     | Arthralgia, non‑erosive arthritis | Arthritis (large joints)       | None                          | Asymmetric oligoarthritis (knees/ankles) | Arthralgia/arthritis, Jaccoud’s      |
| GI involvement        | Deep, punched-out ileocecal ulcers, risk of perforation | Transmural inflammation, strictures, fistulae | None (oral only) | None                         | Serositis, but not ulcerative        |
| Vessel disease        | Both arterial and venous: aneurysm + thrombosis | Rare (thromboembolism) | None                          | None                          | Antiphospholipid syndrome: thrombosis only (no arterial aneurysm) |
| Key diagnostic test   | Pathergy test, HLA-B51           | Colonoscopy with biopsies (granulomas) | Viral PCR, Tzank smear    | HLA-B27 (positive)               | ANA, anti-dsDNA                      |
| Key differentiator    | Hypopyon uveitis + pathergy     | Granulomas on biopsy, fistulae | Painful vesicles, no recurrence of eye | Urethritis + conjunctivitis + arthritis | Anti-dsDNA, low complement, malar rash |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication                  | How it presents                 | Key signs                           | What to do                                                                 |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Blindness** (from uveitis/retinal vasculitis) | Painless loss of vision over weeks (posterior uveitis) or painful red eye (anterior) | Hypopyon, vitreous haze, retinal haemorrhages, neovascularization | Urgent ophthalmology; high-dose steroids + immunosuppressant. Prevent by regular eye exams. |
| **Cerebral venous thrombosis** (dural sinus thrombosis) | Headache, papilloedema, seizures | Empty delta sign on CT/MRV, raised opening pressure on LP | Anticoagulation? (debated) + steroids + immunosuppression. Call neurology. |
| **Parenchymal neuro-Behçet** (brainstem stroke mimic) | Acute hemiparesis, cranial nerve palsies, ataxia | MRI: T2 hyperintensity in brainstem, basal ganglia, internal capsule | IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily × 5, then oral taper. Cyclophosphamide if severe. |
| **Arterial aneurysm** (aorta, popliteal, pulmonary) | Pulsatile mass, sudden pain, haemoptysis (pulmonary aneurysm) | CTA shows saccular aneurysm | Urgent vascular surgery (endovascular or open). Immunosuppress to prevent recurrence. |
| **GI perforation** (deep ileocecal ulcer) | Acute abdomen, peritonism | Free air on abdominal X-ray or CT | Emergency laparotomy + resection. Biopsy shows vasculitis. Start steroids + infliximab. |
| **Large vessel thrombosis** (DVT, vena cava, hepatic veins (Budd‑Chiari)) | Unilateral leg swelling, jaundice/ascites (Budd‑Chiari) | Doppler US: thrombus. Budd‑Chiari: enlarged caudate lobe | Immunosuppression (steroids + azathioprine) +/- anticoagulation with caution. Avoid warfarin without anti-TNF? |

**Late‑presentation pattern:** The patient who “always had mouth ulcers” and now presents with a massive pulmonary aneurysm or blindness. The key is to **ask about recurrent oral/genital ulcers** in any young patient with cryptogenic stroke, uveitis, or pulmonary artery aneurysm.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: “Behçet – Oral, Genital, Eyes, Skin, Joints, Vascular, Neuro”**  
(Think “OGE JSVN” – like “Oh Gee, JSVN?” – not great but memorable.)

**“The 4 Ps of Behçet”**
- P: **P**athergy test
- P: **P**yoderma (pustules)
- P: **P**seudo‑Crohn’s (GI)
- P: **P**hotophobia (uveitis)

**Pearls (exam/ward):**

1. **Always ask about genital ulcers – patients rarely volunteer them.** If a young man has a “red eye” and “mouth ulcers”, ask: “Any sores in your private area?” – a positive answer is a major clue.
2. **The pathergy test is cheap, safe, and specific.** Take a sterile 20G needle, scratch the forearm skin, look for pustule in 48h. Positive in 60–70% (higher in Turkish patients). False negative in Northern Europeans – don’t rely on it to exclude.
3. **In the uveitis patient, do NOT start antibiotics or antiviral** – the hypopyon is sterile. You’ll get culture-negative results and delay proper steroid therapy.
4. **Beware the bleeding aneurysm.** A young patient with Behçet and sudden back pain or haemoptysis → think of arterial aneurysm. Do not give thrombolysis – it can be fatal.
5. **Neuro-Behçet vs. Multiple sclerosis:** Both can cause brainstem lesions, but MS often has periventricular plaques and oligoclonal bands; Behçet lesions are more often in the basal ganglia and have no oligoclonal bands. Also, Behçet has systemic features.
6. **Pregnancy is not contraindication** – colchicine and even azathioprine are used. But avoid cyclophosphamide and mycophenolate.
7. **Do not miss the GI variant in a patient with “IBD” who also has oral ulcers and a pathergy-positive skin test.** A colonic biopsy may show vasculitis, not granulomas.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Assuming all oral ulcers are simple aphthae – ask about recurrence and associated eye/skin/GU symptoms.
- Starting anticoagulation for DVT without giving steroids first → risk of aneurysm rupture.
- Using NSAIDs in Behçet patients with GI involvement → perforation.
- Not checking G6PD before dapsone therapy.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| When to escalate                  | Who to call                  | Specific thresholds                                                                 |
|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Acute loss of vision (sudden)** | Senior ophthalmology (on-call registrar) | –                                                                                    |
| **Headache + papilloedema**       | Neurology (+/- interventional radiology) | Consider MR venogram; if signs of raised ICP, call neurosurgery for LP or shunt.   |
| **Focal neurology (hemiparesis, aphasia)** | Stroke team / neurology     | NIHSS >5 or GCS <13 → activate stroke pathway (but remember it’s inflammatory, not ischaemic – no thrombolysis without ruling out aneurysm). |
| **Pulsatile abdominal mass / back pain** | Vascular surgery             | NEWS2 ≥ 3, lactate >2, hypotension (SBP <90) → call crash team / ICU.              |
| **Acute abdomen (peritonism)**   | General surgery              | Free air on X-ray: call theatre immediately. Lactate >4, GCS drop → ICU.            |
| **Massive haemoptysis**          | Respiratory + ICU            | Consider pulmonary artery aneurysm. Evaluate with CTPA – if ruptured, bronchial artery embolisation or urgent surgery. |
| **Deteriorating conscious state / seizures** | ITU / neurology             | GCS <12, new seizures, airway compromise → call anaesthetics/ICU.                   |
| **Suspected new arterial aneurysm** | Vascular surgery           | Immediate imaging (CTA). If ruptured, activate major haemorrhage protocol.          |

**General threshold for calling seniors:**
- Any new neurological symptom (vision change, weakness, seizure).
- Uveitis not resolving after 48 hours of topical therapy.
- Suspected large vessel thrombosis (Budd‑Chiari, vena cava syndrome) – need hepatology/vascular.
- Refractory disease on first-line treatment (colchicine/azathioprine) – consider anti-TNF, discuss with rheumatology.

**Remember:** Behçet can kill by aneurysm rupture or CNS vasculitis. The key to escalation is **recognising the pattern early** and getting the right specialist involved (ophthalmology, rheumatology, neurology, vascular surgery). Do not hesitate.

# Behçet Disease

**One-liner:** Systemic vasculitis of unknown cause, classically in young adults along the “Silk Road,” presenting with recurrent oral/genital ulcers, uveitis, and skin lesions – a great mimic that can blind or burst.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 28-year-old man of Turkish descent presents to the eye casualty with acute red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision. On closer questioning, he reports recurrent painful mouth ulcers (“canker sores” that come every few months) and occasional painful “sores” on his scrotum that heal with white scarring. He also noticed erythematous tender nodules on his shins last month. Fluorescein exam shows anterior uveitis with hypopyon. A pathergy test (skin prick with a sterile needle) produces a sterile pustule at 48 hours.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- Young adult (20-40 years), Mediterranean/Middle Eastern ancestry → **high pre-test probability**; Silk Road geography is a classic clue.
- Acute red eye + hypopyon (pus in anterior chamber) → **non‑infectious uveitis** – Behçet is one of the few causes of hypopyon without infection.
- Recurrent painful mouth ulcers (oral aphthae) – **major diagnostic criterion**; look for multiple, deep, well-defined ulcers (not the trivial ones you see in common aphthous).
- Genital ulcers (scrotal, labial) – **second major criterion**; often deeper than oral ones, heal with scarring (unlike herpes).
- Erythema nodosum on shins → **skin manifestation** of Behçet – painful, red, subcutaneous nodules, not nodules of erythema induratum.
- Positive pathergy test → **specific but not sensitive** hyper-reactivity of skin to minor trauma; a key pattern clue in the right context.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Neurologic variant (Neuro-Behçet):** A 35-year-old with headache, hemiparesis, and meningism – looks like stroke or meningitis. The clue is a history of recurrent oral ulcers that the patient “always had.” Imaging shows brainstem or basal ganglia lesions.
2. **Vascular variant:** A 30-year-old with unilateral leg swelling (DVT) or a pulsatile abdominal mass (abdominal aortic aneurysm). Behçet is one of the few causes of both arterial aneurysm and venous thrombosis. Clue: recurrent oral ulcers and positive pathergy.
3. **GI variant:** A 25-year-old with abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and mouth ulcers – mimics Crohn’s. However, Behçet ulcers are usually deeper, punched-out, and more likely to perforate. Look for other organ involvement (eyes, skin, joints).

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**
Behçet disease is a chronic, relapsing‑remitting systemic vasculitis that can inflame any size blood vessel (arteries and veins, small to large). It is not a classic autoimmune disease (no autoantibodies) but an **autoinflammatory** condition driven by a hyperactive innate immune system attacking the vessel wall.

- **Frequency:** Rare in northern Europe, common in Turkey (prevalence ~400/100,000), but you will see it in diverse populations due to migration patterns. Miss it and the patient can become blind (untreated uveitis) or develop fatal vascular rupture.
- **Stakes:** Blindness (leading cause of morbidity), CNS involvement (parenchymal disease or dural sinus thrombosis), large-vessel aneurysms (rupture), and GI perforation.
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking oral ulcers for “aphthous stomatitis” (common), missing genital ulcers because patients don’t volunteer them, and treating “red eye” as conjunctivitis while missing uveitis.

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:**
Behçet is a **neutrophilic vasculitis** – the neutrophil is the main effector cell. There is a genetic predisposition (HLA‑B51 is the strongest risk allele, particularly in Silk Road populations). Environmental triggers (e.g., oral streptococcal antigens, herpes simplex virus, heat shock proteins) are thought to initiate an exaggerated inflammatory response in genetically susceptible individuals.

**Mechanism → sign:**

- **Oral/genital ulcers:** Activated T cells (Th1/Th17) and neutrophils infiltrate the mucosa. The over‑exuberant release of cytokines (TNF‑α, IL‑1, IL‑17) causes deep, painful ulcerations. Why so recurrent? The abnormal immune response never fully switches off → relapsing course.
- **Uveitis (especially hypopyon):** Neutrophils migrate into the anterior chamber → pus (hypopyon). This is a **sterile** inflammation – don’t try to culture it; it’s an attack on the uveal tract.
- **Skin lesions:** Pathergy – the exaggerated response to needle prick reflects the skin’s hyperreactivity: neutrophils rush to the trauma site and produce a pustule. Erythema nodosum results from septal panniculitis (inflammation of fat septa). Papulopustular lesions (acneiform) are not from Propionibacterium but from sterile neutrophilic infiltration.
- **Vascular involvement:** The vasa vasorum is attacked → vessel wall weakened → aneurysm (arterial) or intimal inflammation → thrombosis (venous). The **coexistence** of arterial aneurysm and venous thrombosis is a hallmark that helps differentiate from conventional DVT (where arterial disease is rare).
- **CNS involvement:** Small‑vessel vasculitis in the brainstem, basal ganglia, white matter → focal neurological deficits. Meningeal inflammation → headache, neck stiffness (but CSF is usually bland with neutrophils).
- **GI involvement:** Vasculitic ulcers in the intestine (especially ileocecal) resemble Crohn’s but are deeper, punched‑out, and more likely to perforate (because the vessel wall is directly damaged).

**Connecting to pattern clues:**
- The hypopyon uveitis is a direct consequence of neutrophil chemotaxis into the eye.
- The pathergy test is a recapitulation of the skin’s hypersensitive neutrophil response.
- Recurrent oral/genital ulcers are the “low‑grade hum” of chronic TNF‑α activation that flares cyclically.

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Cause / Trigger      | Category          | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| HLA‑B51 positivity   | Genetic (strong)  | “Patient of Turkish/Japanese/Middle Eastern origin” |
| Family history       | Familial          | “Sibling also has recurrent mouth ulcers and iritis” |
| Oral streptococcal infection (e.g., Streptococcus sanguinis) | Infectious trigger | “Had a dental procedure 2–4 weeks before flare” |
| HSV infection        | Viral trigger     | “Recurrent cold sores and then developed genital ulcers” |
| Heat shock proteins  | Stress / immune   | “Flare after major surgery or fever” |
| Smoking              | Environmental     | “Symptoms worse when smoking” (but effect is controversial) |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic, but with a strong genetic component (HLA‑B51 positive in ~50–80% of patients, depending on ethnicity). No single trigger is identified in most cases; it’s a **multifactorial** interplay.

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (International Study Group):**
- **Required:** Recurrent oral aphthous ulcers (≥3 episodes in 12 months)
- **Plus any 2 of:**
  1. Recurrent genital ulceration
  2. Eye lesions (anterior/posterior uveitis, retinal vasculitis)
  3. Skin lesions (erythema nodosum, papulopustular lesions, pathergy positivity)
  4. Positive pathergy test (sterile pustule at 48h after needle prick)

No single lab test confirms it – it’s clinical.

| Test                     | What it shows                   | Why you order it                          | How to interpret                                                                 |
|--------------------------|---------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Pathergy test            | Skin hyperreactivity            | Specific (but sensitivity variable)       | Pustule at 48h → positive. Note: very low sensitivity in Northern Europeans.     |
| Ophthalmoscopy/slit lamp | Uveitis, retinal vasculitis     | To detect asymptomatic eye involvement    | Hypopyon = acute anterior uveitis. Retinal haemorrhages = vasculitis.            |
| MRI brain (if neuro sx)  | Brainstem/basal ganglia lesions | To diagnose Neuro-Behçet                  | T2 hyperintensities in midbrain, pons, basal ganglia (not typical MS pattern).   |
| MRV (if headache)        | Dural sinus thrombosis          | Suspect CNS venous occlusion              | Empty delta sign on MRV = superior sagittal sinus thrombosis.                    |
| HLA-B51                  | Genetic risk marker             | Supports diagnosis but not confirmatory   | Positive in ~50% of patients; negative does NOT rule out.                        |
| ESR/CRP                  | Inflammation                    | Monitor disease activity                  | Usually very elevated during flares.                                             |
| ANCA / ANA / dsDNA       | Exclusion                      | Rule out GPA (ANCA) and SLE (ANA/dsDNA)   | Negative in Behçet (helps differential).                                         |

**What single finding confirms?** None in isolation. The combination of oral ulcers + genital ulcers + uveitis is pathognomonic. **Pathergy positivity** in a patient with uveitis is highly specific.

**What single finding rules out?** A negative pathergy test does not rule out – only about 60% of patients (in Turkey) are positive. Lack of eye involvement in a patient with only mucocutaneous disease does not rule out Behçet, but it shifts the diagnosis away if more than a few years without eye inflammation.

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **If acute uveitis (painful red eye, hypopyon):** Urgent ophthalmology referral. Topical corticosteroid (e.g., prednisolone acetate 1% one drop hourly) and cycloplegic (atropine 1% to prevent synechiae). **Do not** give antibiotics – it’s sterile.
- **If suspected arterial aneurysm (painful pulsatile mass, back pain):** Immediate vascular surgery consultation. CTA or ultrasound. **Do not** start anticoagulation – risk of rupture.
- **If suspected acute CNS vasculitis (headache, focal deficit, meningism):** Urgent MRI/MRV. Start IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily × 5 days.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Mucocutaneous disease (oral/genital ulcers, skin lesions):**
  - Colchicine 500–600 mcg PO BD – first line, reduces frequency. (Mechanism: inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis.)
  - Topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% paste for oral ulcers, clobetasol cream for genital).
  - If colchicine fails: dapsone 50–100 mg PO daily (check G6PD) or azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day.
- **Active eye disease (uveitis, retinal vasculitis):**
  - Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 2–4 weeks) plus a steroid-sparing agent.
  - Azathioprine 75–100 mg PO daily (2 mg/kg) is first-line for eye disease. Cyclosporine A (3–5 mg/kg/day) or TNF inhibitors (infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then q8weeks) for refractory cases.
- **Major vessel thrombosis (DVT, dural sinus thrombosis):**
  - **Controversial area.** Inflammation-driven thrombus → anticoagulation alone is insufficient. Treat with steroids (prednisolone 40–60 mg/day) and immunosuppressants (azathioprine or cyclophosphamide). Anticoagulation is debated – current guidelines recommend immunosuppression first; add anticoagulation only if no aneurysm and with caution.
- **GI involvement (pain, diarrhoea, ulcers):**
  - Corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day). Anti-TNF (infliximab) is effective. **Avoid NSAIDs** – they can cause perforation.

**Long-term:**
- **Maintenance:** Colchicine for mucocutaneous. For organ-threatening disease (eye, CNS, vasculature), maintain on azathioprine + low-dose prednisolone. The goal is to wean steroids to <10 mg/day by 3–6 months.
- **Anti-TNF agents** (infliximab, adalimumab) are second/third line but effective for all severe manifestations.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - **Start anticoagulation for DVT without first controlling inflammation** – risk of paradoxical rupture of arterial aneurysms.
  - **Use high-dose steroids for months without steroid-sparing** – avoid osteopenia, avascular necrosis.
  - **Prescribe colchicine in renal failure without dose adjustment** – toxicity (diarrhoea, bone marrow suppression).
- **Pregnancy:** Disease often improves. Colchicine is safe. Azathioprine is used with caution. Avoid mycophenolate and cyclophosphamide.

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature               | Behçet                          | Crohn’s disease (GI)           | Herpes simplex (genital)      | Reactive arthritis (Reiter’s)   | Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Oral ulcers           | Painful, recurrent, multiple    | Painful, deep aphthous-like    | Painful vesicles on lips      | Shallow, painless?               | Typically painless, “lupus” ulcers |
| Genital ulcers        | Deep, scar-forming              | Perianal fissures/fistulas     | Clusters of vesicular ulcers  | Usually none (balanitis circinata)| Rare                                |
| Eye involvement       | Anterior uveitis, hypopyon, retinal vasculitis | Episcleritis (rare) | Keratoconjunctivitis (dendritic) | Conjunctivitis, iritis      | Retinal vasculitis, but no hypopyon |
| Skin lesions          | Erythema nodosum, papulopustular, pathergy | Pyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum | Vesicular rash | Keratoderma blenorrhagicum, circinate balanitis | Malar rash, discoid, photosensitivity |
| Joint involvement     | Arthralgia, non‑erosive arthritis | Arthritis (large joints)       | None                          | Asymmetric oligoarthritis (knees/ankles) | Arthralgia/arthritis, Jaccoud’s      |
| GI involvement        | Deep, punched-out ileocecal ulcers, risk of perforation | Transmural inflammation, strictures, fistulae | None (oral only) | None                         | Serositis, but not ulcerative        |
| Vessel disease        | Both arterial and venous: aneurysm + thrombosis | Rare (thromboembolism) | None                          | None                          | Antiphospholipid syndrome: thrombosis only (no arterial aneurysm) |
| Key diagnostic test   | Pathergy test, HLA-B51           | Colonoscopy with biopsies (granulomas) | Viral PCR, Tzank smear    | HLA-B27 (positive)               | ANA, anti-dsDNA                      |
| Key differentiator    | Hypopyon uveitis + pathergy     | Granulomas on biopsy, fistulae | Painful vesicles, no recurrence of eye | Urethritis + conjunctivitis + arthritis | Anti-dsDNA, low complement, malar rash |

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication                  | How it presents                 | Key signs                           | What to do                                                                 |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Blindness** (from uveitis/retinal vasculitis) | Painless loss of vision over weeks (posterior uveitis) or painful red eye (anterior) | Hypopyon, vitreous haze, retinal haemorrhages, neovascularization | Urgent ophthalmology; high-dose steroids + immunosuppressant. Prevent by regular eye exams. |
| **Cerebral venous thrombosis** (dural sinus thrombosis) | Headache, papilloedema, seizures | Empty delta sign on CT/MRV, raised opening pressure on LP | Anticoagulation? (debated) + steroids + immunosuppression. Call neurology. |
| **Parenchymal neuro-Behçet** (brainstem stroke mimic) | Acute hemiparesis, cranial nerve palsies, ataxia | MRI: T2 hyperintensity in brainstem, basal ganglia, internal capsule | IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily × 5, then oral taper. Cyclophosphamide if severe. |
| **Arterial aneurysm** (aorta, popliteal, pulmonary) | Pulsatile mass, sudden pain, haemoptysis (pulmonary aneurysm) | CTA shows saccular aneurysm | Urgent vascular surgery (endovascular or open). Immunosuppress to prevent recurrence. |
| **GI perforation** (deep ileocecal ulcer) | Acute abdomen, peritonism | Free air on abdominal X-ray or CT | Emergency laparotomy + resection. Biopsy shows vasculitis. Start steroids + infliximab. |
| **Large vessel thrombosis** (DVT, vena cava, hepatic veins (Budd‑Chiari)) | Unilateral leg swelling, jaundice/ascites (Budd‑Chiari) | Doppler US: thrombus. Budd‑Chiari: enlarged caudate lobe | Immunosuppression (steroids + azathioprine) +/- anticoagulation with caution. Avoid warfarin without anti-TNF? |

**Late‑presentation pattern:** The patient who “always had mouth ulcers” and now presents with a massive pulmonary aneurysm or blindness. The key is to **ask about recurrent oral/genital ulcers** in any young patient with cryptogenic stroke, uveitis, or pulmonary artery aneurysm.

---

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: “Behçet – Oral, Genital, Eyes, Skin, Joints, Vascular, Neuro”**  
(Think “OGE JSVN” – like “Oh Gee, JSVN?” – not great but memorable.)

**“The 4 Ps of Behçet”**
- P: **P**athergy test
- P: **P**yoderma (pustules)
- P: **P**seudo‑Crohn’s (GI)
- P: **P**hotophobia (uveitis)

**Pearls (exam/ward):**

1. **Always ask about genital ulcers – patients rarely volunteer them.** If a young man has a “red eye” and “mouth ulcers”, ask: “Any sores in your private area?” – a positive answer is a major clue.
2. **The pathergy test is cheap, safe, and specific.** Take a sterile 20G needle, scratch the forearm skin, look for pustule in 48h. Positive in 60–70% (higher in Turkish patients). False negative in Northern Europeans – don’t rely on it to exclude.
3. **In the uveitis patient, do NOT start antibiotics or antiviral** – the hypopyon is sterile. You’ll get culture-negative results and delay proper steroid therapy.
4. **Beware the bleeding aneurysm.** A young patient with Behçet and sudden back pain or haemoptysis → think of arterial aneurysm. Do not give thrombolysis – it can be fatal.
5. **Neuro-Behçet vs. Multiple sclerosis:** Both can cause brainstem lesions, but MS often has periventricular plaques and oligoclonal bands; Behçet lesions are more often in the basal ganglia and have no oligoclonal bands. Also, Behçet has systemic features.
6. **Pregnancy is not contraindication** – colchicine and even azathioprine are used. But avoid cyclophosphamide and mycophenolate.
7. **Do not miss the GI variant in a patient with “IBD” who also has oral ulcers and a pathergy-positive skin test.** A colonic biopsy may show vasculitis, not granulomas.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Assuming all oral ulcers are simple aphthae – ask about recurrence and associated eye/skin/GU symptoms.
- Starting anticoagulation for DVT without giving steroids first → risk of aneurysm rupture.
- Using NSAIDs in Behçet patients with GI involvement → perforation.
- Not checking G6PD before dapsone therapy.

---

## 10. Escalation Criteria

| When to escalate                  | Who to call                  | Specific thresholds                                                                 |
|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Acute loss of vision (sudden)** | Senior ophthalmology (on-call registrar) | –                                                                                    |
| **Headache + papilloedema**       | Neurology (+/- interventional radiology) | Consider MR venogram; if signs of raised ICP, call neurosurgery for LP or shunt.   |
| **Focal neurology (hemiparesis, aphasia)** | Stroke team / neurology     | NIHSS >5 or GCS <13 → activate stroke pathway (but remember it’s inflammatory, not ischaemic – no thrombolysis without ruling out aneurysm). |
| **Pulsatile abdominal mass / back pain** | Vascular surgery             | NEWS2 ≥ 3, lactate >2, hypotension (SBP <90) → call crash team / ICU.              |
| **Acute abdomen (peritonism)**   | General surgery              | Free air on X-ray: call theatre immediately. Lactate >4, GCS drop → ICU.            |
| **Massive haemoptysis**          | Respiratory + ICU            | Consider pulmonary artery aneurysm. Evaluate with CTPA – if ruptured, bronchial artery embolisation or urgent surgery. |
| **Deteriorating conscious state / seizures** | ITU / neurology             | GCS <12, new seizures, airway compromise → call anaesthetics/ICU.                   |
| **Suspected new arterial aneurysm** | Vascular surgery           | Immediate imaging (CTA). If ruptured, activate major haemorrhage protocol.          |

**General threshold for calling seniors:**
- Any new neurological symptom (vision change, weakness, seizure).
- Uveitis not resolving after 48 hours of topical therapy.
- Suspected large vessel thrombosis (Budd‑Chiari, vena cava syndrome) – need hepatology/vascular.
- Refractory disease on first-line treatment (colchicine/azathioprine) – consider anti-TNF, discuss with rheumatology.

**Remember:** Behçet can kill by aneurysm rupture or CNS vasculitis. The key to escalation is **recognising the pattern early** and getting the right specialist involved (ophthalmology, rheumatology, neurology, vascular surgery). Do not hesitate.
